rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tertiary carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (H2SO4) catalysis. This carbocation is then trapped by a simple nitrile (acetonitrile) to form a nitrilium ion intermediate, which upon hydrolysis yields the corresponding N-tert-butylacetamide. All components are simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of an alcohol and a nitrile into an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, non-aromatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It specifically avoids complex carbocation sources, alkenes (which could suggest hydroamination or other additions), or functional groups that might trigger unrelated transformations like Beckmann rearrangement (oximes) or Friedel-Crafts reactions (arenes).
